column2: I have recently bought a medium sized plot of wood/scrub land. Half of this plot was once used to dump shale from coal mines, which means I have a lot of shale.    I have been wondering/looking for possible uses for the stuff. One thought I've had is to make "mud bricks" however I can't find any mention of whether it is possible or not. I know the traditional method uses loamy soil but would it work with shale?    I don't have a kiln or any way of baking the bricks, so would be reliant on the sun. I was thinking maybe adding water and maybe some loam?    Aside: there are no suitable tags for my question and I do not have the reputation to add any, I'm thinking I could be on the wrong site for this question, if I am, please advise me of a more suitable one. Sun-dried mud bricks are traditionally made from a mix of loamy soil, water, and sometimes straw. Shale, which is a sedimentary rock formed from clay, can be more challenging to use because it is generally hard and does not bind well on its own. If the shale on your plot is broken down into a fine powder or soil-like consistency, you could try mixing it with loamy soil and water to form bricks. I was wondering if we could possibly reduce the severity of, or steer a hurricane by changing albedo (e.g. dying the part ocean with a temporary light colored dye)? My thought would be to put this dye well in front of the path of a hurricane, with enough time to reflect the sunlight back off the ocean and cool the ocean surface. When the hurricane arrives there, the ocean surface should then be cooler, leading to the hurricane being less powerful. I am also wondering if this could be used to steer the hurricane somehow (e.g. if a hurricane would be drawn to or repelled away from cool ocean surface). If so, could we use that to steer a hurricane away from populated areas?    Some potential methods (there may be others):    Use light colored dye injected into the ocean    Use light colored smoke to block the sun from reaching the ocean    Possibly use "cloud seeding" ahead of the hurricane and form clouds to block the sun light from warming the ocean. - Covered in the alternate question    Perhaps a "surface film" could also be applied temporarily to part of the ocean (sort of like a "light colored oil spill", hopefully with something safer for the environment than oil). - Covered in the alternate question    If this experiment were to be conducted, I would think it should be done far from inhabited land. Environmental impacts would need to be thought out too, e.g. would a film block the carbon dioxide / oxygen exchange in that area and cause severe environmental impact? Could the experiment actually strengthen instead of weaken a hurricane? Could the experiment actually strengthen instead of weaken a hurricane? Applying light-colored dyes or films to the ocean to alter hurricane intensity or path could present several environmental risks and unintended consequences. Potential impacts include disrupting marine ecosystems, impairing gas exchange (which can affect oxygen and carbon dioxide balances), and introducing pollutants. While in theory, increasing the albedo of the ocean surface might locally reduce sea surface temperatures and potentially limit hurricane intensity, such interventions would require immense scale to have any measurable effect. Current scientific consensus is that the practical, technological, and environmental challenges far outweigh any likely benefit, and manipulating hurricane paths in this way is not currently feasible.

My question is simple: is there a website or institute that has integrated statistics on forecasting the occurrence of rainbows in different countries around the world? Currently, there is no global meteorological organization or website that provides integrated statistics or forecasts specifically for the occurrence of rainbows in different countries. Rainbows are local and depend on specific weather conditions (such as sunlight and rain), so they are not typically tracked or forecasted in the same way as other weather phenomena. For more information, you might look at weather-specific forums, photography websites, or scientific publications discussing atmospheric optics, but there is no centralized institute for global rainbow occurrence statistics.

I would like to know what would happen with the atmosphere of Venus when it gets tidally locked, i.e. when one side would perpetually face the Sun.  Probably a thermal low would be at the subsolar region then.    At the surface Venus has an atmospheric pressure of 92 atm. and a temperature of 462⁰   C.    Are there General Circulation Models or Global Climate Models that can handle such input parameters ?    If so, are there GCM users (groups, universities etc.) who could run a tidally locked Venus simulation ? There are General Circulation Models (GCMs) and Global Climate Models developed for planetary atmospheres that can be adapted to handle Venus-like conditions, including high surface pressures (92 atm) and temperatures (~462°C). Some Earth-based GCMs have been modified to simulate Venus’s climate, such as NASA's ROCKE-3D, the UK Met Office Unified Model, and the Institut Pierre-Simon Laplace (IPSL) Venus GCM. These models are capable of exploring scenarios like tidal locking and can simulate atmospheric circulation under such extreme conditions. Research groups and institutions such as NASA GISS

To indicate the presence of multicellular life on Mars, scientists look for specific chemical markers or biosignatures that are typically produced by complex organisms. These could include complex organic molecules (such as lipids unique to multicellular life), patterns of fossilized structures resembling those formed by multicellular organisms, or stromatolite-like formations. Additionally, scientists search for environments with liquid water—such as ancient lake beds, underground aquifers, or recurring slope lineae—since these could support or preserve multicellular life. Yes, there are several R libraries designed for structural geology that include Schmidt net (stereonet) plotting. The most notable package is 'RFOC', which allows you to plot and analyze orientation data on stereonets within R. Another useful package is 'struct3D', which also provides tools for working with orientation data. These packages allow you to integrate geometric plotting with R's statistical and data handling capabilities, providing more flexibility compared to standalone software.

The apparent absence of significant iron deposits in New Guinea, despite the presence of valuable metals like copper, gold, nickel, and cobalt, can be attributed to its unique geology. New Guinea's mineral wealth primarily originates from tectonic activity related to subduction zones, which facilitates the formation of porphyry copper and epithermal gold deposits—these processes are also favorable for nickel and cobalt enrichment. What events took place in our Solar System which led to formation of the Earth? I have heard from sources that a big giant star exploded and the Earth was formed. Is this true ? What events took place in our Solar System which led to formation of the Earth? I have heard from sources that a big giant star exploded and the Earth was formed. Is this true ? The Earth and the rest of the Solar System formed about 4.6 billion years ago from a giant cloud of gas and dust called the solar nebula. A nearby massive star may have exploded as a supernova, and its shockwave possibly triggered the collapse of this nebula. As the nebula collapsed under gravity, it spun and flattened into a disk. Most of the material gathered in the center to form the Sun, while the rest gradually stuck together to form planets, including Earth, as well as moons, asteroids, and other bodies. Is there any scientific value to understanding soundscapes on a global basis? Yes, there is scientific value to understanding soundscapes on a global basis. Studying soundscapes worldwide can help scientists monitor biodiversity, observe changes in animal behavior, detect the impact of human activities, and track environmental changes such as deforestation or urbanization. Collecting such data from diverse regions can provide a broader picture of ecosystem health and inform conservation strategies, as well as help policymakers design measures to mitigate noise pollution and protect natural habitats.

While earthquake magnitude (such as 8.0) is a fixed value describing the energy released at the source, the shaking felt at a distance depends on several factors, including the distance from the epicenter, depth of the quake, and local geology. To estimate how strong the shaking would feel at your location, you can use the Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI) scale, which describes the observed effects. The intensity generally decreases with distance from the source. There are online calculators and attenuation relationships (such as the USGS's ShakeMap or empirical attenuation equations) that can give a rough estimate

For some time now, I've noticed that mountains in southeast Asia seem to be unusually "jagged" with steep surfaces. My basic knowledge of how mountains are formed (via geologic processes) tells me that it's more common to find mountains like these in glacial areas (e.g. northern Canada, Norway, etc.), whereas those formed by tectonic plate collisions appear more "traditional" (e.g. main range of Himalayas, Andes, etc.), and geologically old mountains have a weathered appearance (e.g. Appalachians).    What caused/causes these SE Asian mountains to have their distinct appearance? For some time now, I've noticed that mountains in southeast Asia seem to be unusually "jagged" with steep surfaces. My basic knowledge of how mountains are formed (via geologic processes) tells me that it's more common to find mountains like these in glacial areas (e.g. northern Canada, Norway, etc.), whereas those formed by tectonic plate collisions appear more "traditional" (e.g. main range of Himalayas, Andes, etc.), and geologically old mountains have a weathered appearance (e.g. Appalachians).    What caused/causes these SE Asian mountains to have their distinct appearance? For some time now, I've noticed that mountains in southeast Asia seem to be unusually "jagged" with steep surfaces. My basic knowledge of how mountains are formed (via geologic processes) tells me that it's more common to find mountains like these in glacial areas (e.g. northern Canada, Norway, etc.), whereas those formed by tectonic plate collisions appear more "traditional" (e.g. main range of Himalayas, Andes, etc.), and geologically old mountains have a weathered appearance (e.g. Appalachians).    What caused/causes these SE Asian mountains to have their distinct appearance? The steep and jagged mountain landscapes of Southeast Asia are primarily shaped by tectonic activity, especially the collision and movement of the Eurasian, Indian, and Philippine Sea plates. These ongoing tectonic processes uplift and fracture the crust, creating sharp, rugged terrain. Additionally, tropical weather patterns—with heavy rainfall and intense weathering—erode the rock differently than glacial regions, further contributing to the jaggedness. Unlike mountains shaped largely by glacial carving, Southeast Asian mountains are formed and sculpted by a combination of rapid uplift and aggressive tropical erosion, resulting in their distinct steep and dramatic appearance.

By the end of 2015, it was estimated that there were about 1.5 trillion barrels of crude oil reserves left in the world. Since the mid-19th century, when oil began to be used massively, it is estimated that roughly an equal or larger amount—around 1.3 to 1.4 trillion barrels—had already been consumed worldwide. Therefore, about half (or slightly more) of the world's conventional oil endowment had been used by 2015, with a similar amount remaining at that time.

Why is the sea level in Hudson Bay decreasing so much? Hudson Bay is pretty far up north, much closer to glaciers. Would it make sense for it to recede at this level with sources of fresh water relatively close? Why is the sea level in Hudson Bay decreasing so much? Hudson Bay is pretty far up north, much closer to glaciers. Would it make sense for it to recede at this level with sources of fresh water relatively close? Why is the sea level in Hudson Bay decreasing so much? Hudson Bay is pretty far up north, much closer to glaciers. Would it make sense for it to recede at this level with sources of fresh water relatively close? The decrease in sea level in Hudson Bay is primarily due to 'glacial isostatic adjustment' (GIA). After the last Ice Age, the melting of massive ice sheets that once covered the region has allowed the land to slowly rise—a process known as post-glacial rebound. This uplift of the land surface is occurring faster than the rate of sea level rise from added meltwater, causing local sea levels to fall. Additionally, the melting of glaciers reduces their gravitational pull, which causes water to move away from the immediate vicinity. These combined effects explain why sea level is receding in Hudson Bay,

Are clouds a solid, liquid, or gas?  I have been looking online and they are often described ambiguously as a "mass". For instance, from NASA:  A cloud is a mass of water drops or ice crystals suspended in the atmosphere. Clouds form when water condenses in the sky. The condensation lets us see the water vapor. There are many different types of clouds. Clouds are an important part of Earth’s weather and climate.  Since they describe it as a vapor, that makes me think it is indeed a gas. But condensation is by definition the change of state from a gas to a liquid. So that makes it sound like it could be a liquid, or contain liquid droplets rather than "freely moving" molecules that typically make up a gas.  But at another web site, which doesn't seem all that authoritative, it says that clouds are a solid, liquid, and gas simultaneously!  A cloud is a liquid, a solid, and a gas.  That does seem intriguing. If I remember correctly, liquids are supposed to be incompressible, and clouds are likely compressible. Are clouds a solid, liquid, or gas? When water forms clouds in the atmosphere, it is mostly present as tiny liquid droplets. However, depending on the temperature and altitude, clouds can also contain tiny ice crystals. Water vapor—the gaseous form of water—is present as well, but the visible part of clouds is made up of liquid droplets and/or ice crystals suspended in air. So, clouds are primarily made of liquid water droplets and sometimes solid ice crystals, both suspended in a gas (air).

Messing around on Google Earth recently I noticed a number of striations in the Eastern Pacific. These appear in an East-West orientation and seem to start on the North and South American continental shelves, and extend for roughly half the Pacific Ocean. For example one of these striations start in Santa Rosa Island off California and ends at Hawaii. These striations also appear to be roughly equally spaced at 8 degree intervals. The North and South American striations are angled with respect to each other and seem to converge at roughly Tahiti.  What causes these? I'm a fascinated novice. These appear in an East-West orientation and seem to start on the North and South American continental shelves, and extend for roughly half the Pacific Ocean. For example one of these striations start in Santa Rosa Island off California and ends at Hawaii. These striations also appear to be roughly equally spaced at 8 degree intervals. The North and South American striations are angled with respect to each other and seem to converge at roughly Tahiti.  What causes these? I'm a fascinated novice. The evenly spaced East-West striations you see in the Eastern Pacific are likely 'fracture zones,' which are linear features on the ocean floor formed by plate tectonics. These fracture zones are created along transform faults as tectonic plates move past each other, particularly at spreading centers like the East Pacific Rise. The parallel, regularly spaced lines you observe are the surface expressions of these deep-sea faults and their scarps, which can extend for thousands of kilometers across the ocean floor. These features are visible on satellite maps because they have slight elevation differences that affect the sea surface, which satellites can detect.

If I have wind speeds at 80m and 120m above the surface and I am interested in approximating the speed at 100m, is it valid to just average the 80 and 120 speeds? In other words, should I expect wind speed to be a linear function of height or would a nonlinear function be more appropriate? If I have wind speeds at 80m and 120m above the surface and I am interested in approximating the speed at 100m, is it valid to just average the 80 and 120 speeds? In other words, should I expect wind speed to be a linear function of height or would a nonlinear function be more appropriate? If I have wind speeds at 80m and 120m above the surface and I am interested in approximating the speed at 100m, is it valid to just average the 80 and 120 speeds? In other words, should I expect wind speed to be a linear function of height or would a nonlinear function be more appropriate? Wind speed generally increases with height above the surface, but the relationship is typically nonlinear. Two common models to describe this relationship are the logarithmic wind profile and the power law. Instead of taking a simple average of the wind speeds at 80m and 120m, it's more appropriate to use one of these models to interpolate the wind speed at 100m. The power law, for example, uses the formula: V2 = V1 * (H2/H1)^α, where V1 and V2 are wind speeds at heights H1 and H2, and α is the wind shear

The Egyptian desert consists of sandy soil and this soil type usually lacks the nutrients necessary for crops to grow. So how will this work? Redirecting agricultural waste water into the desert can help transform arid, barren land into fertile, arable soil suitable for agriculture. The key benefit is providing a consistent water source, which is crucial for plant growth. However, simply adding water is not always enough, especially since desert sand often lacks essential nutrients. In Egypt's plan, the nutrient-rich agricultural runoff can also contribute to improving the soil quality over time. Additionally, techniques like adding organic matter, soil amendments, crop rotation, and careful land management will be necessary to successfully convert sandy desert into productive farmland.

According to textbook knowledge, the mass of the earth is about 6×1024kg  . How is this number determined when one cannot just weight the earth using regular scales? According to textbook knowledge, the mass of the earth is about 6×1024kg  . How is this number determined when one cannot just weight the earth using regular scales? According to textbook knowledge, the mass of the earth is about 6×1024kg  . How is this number determined when one cannot just weight the earth using regular scales? The mass of the Earth is estimated using experiments that rely on gravitational principles. The most famous method is the Cavendish experiment, performed in 1798 by Henry Cavendish. In this experiment, the gravitational attraction between known masses was measured, which allowed scientists to determine the gravitational constant (G). Using Newton's law of universal gravitation, along with measurements of the Earth's radius and the acceleration due to gravity at its surface, the Earth's mass can be calculated with the formula: M = gR^2/G, where M is the mass of the Earth, g is the acceleration due to gravity

My question refers to the current process of climate change. CO2 is rising, which leads to the greenhouse effect, which raises temperatures. This leads to more wildfires, which reduces number of trees, increasing CO2 and reducing CO2 capacity absorption. Ice caps start to melt, which reduces sunlight reflection (less snow), trapping more heat on atmosphere. Water rises, taking over land and trees, further enhancing the CO2 absorption capacity. Oceans acidify, lowering their CO2 absorption capacity too. Etc etc.    It seems the process of climate change is a "vicious circle", with a lot of feedback loops reinforcing the trends. Is this the case? Are there counteracting forces that go against this circle? Yes, the process of climate change involves several positive feedback loops that reinforce warming trends, such as increased wildfires reducing tree cover (and CO2 absorption), melting ice reducing the Earth's reflectivity, and ocean acidification limiting oceanic CO2 uptake. However, there are also some counteracting or negative feedback mechanisms, for example: higher atmospheric CO2 can cause some plants to grow faster and absorb more CO2; increased cloud cover could reflect more sunlight back into space; and warmer temperatures might increase weathering rates of rocks, which can slowly remove CO2 from the atmosphere. These negative feedbacks can help

However, I have not heard whether there is any actual physical evidence on Earth that points to a prior collision. Is there geological (or other physical) evidence here on Earth that confirms the moon originated from a collision on Earth? If so what is that evidence? However, I have not heard whether there is any actual physical evidence on Earth that points to a prior collision. Is there geological (or other physical) evidence here on Earth that confirms the moon originated from a collision on Earth? If so what is that evidence? I've seen models in astronomy that show how the Earth-Moon system must have come together after a collision. However, I have not heard whether there is any actual physical evidence on Earth that points to a prior collision. Is there geological (or other physical) evidence here on Earth that confirms the moon originated from a collision on Earth? If so what is that evidence? There is geological and chemical evidence on Earth that supports the giant impact hypothesis for the Moon's formation. One of the strongest pieces of evidence is the remarkable similarity in isotopic compositions—especially of oxygen, silicon, and titanium—between Earth rocks and lunar samples. This suggests that the material that makes up both bodies came from a common source or underwent thorough mixing during a massive collision. Additionally, there are indications of changes in the distribution of certain elements in Earth's mantle, such as a depletion in siderophile (iron-loving) elements, which is consistent with a large impact event stripping away part of Earth's outer layers

According to a link from a comment in this question, Titan might have one cell going from north to south poles.  What factors affect the number of cells a planet has, and how? Or to put it another way, given a hypothetical planet with atmosphere, what facts would you need to know in order to guess at how many cells it has? A planet’s rotation speed has a significant impact on the number of atmospheric cells it has. Faster rotation creates stronger Coriolis forces, which tend to break up the circulation into more cells per hemisphere. Other important factors include the size (radius) of the planet, the thickness and composition of the atmosphere, and the amount of energy the planet receives from its star (insolation). To estimate the number of cells a hypothetical planet would have, you would need to know its rotation rate, atmospheric depth, size, and the intensity of stellar heating.

See this map of tropical cyclone activity and note the lack of activity in the south Atlantic and also in the south Pacific until you near Australia.    Where are all the tropical cyclones in the southern Atlantic basin? Tropical cyclones are extremely rare in the southern Atlantic primarily due to several unfavorable environmental factors. These include cooler sea surface temperatures, higher vertical wind shear, and less atmospheric instability compared to more active regions like the North Atlantic or western Pacific. As a result, the conditions necessary for cyclone formation are rarely met, which explains the near absence of storms in the southern Atlantic basin.

When will it start to move faster and how would a compass behave? Magnetic pole reversals, also known as geomagnetic reversals, occur due to changes and instabilities in the flow of molten iron and other metals in the Earth's outer core. The reversal itself can take thousands to several tens of thousands of years to complete. During a reversal, the strength of the magnetic field decreases, and the poles may wander and even temporarily exist in multiple locations before settling into the new orientation. While Earth's solid core does not flip, the movement of molten material in the outer core drives the change in the magnetic field direction. Environmental changes might include a temporary reduction in the field’s strength

I have never understood why earth's inner core is solid. Considering that the inner core is made of an iron-nickel alloy (melting point around 1350 C to 1600 C) and the temperature of the inner core is approximately 5430 C (about the temperature of the surface of the sun). Since Earth's core is nearly 3-4 times the melting point of iron-nickel alloys how can it possibly be solid? I have never understood why earth's inner core is solid. Since Earth's core is nearly 3-4 times the melting point of iron-nickel alloys how can it possibly be solid? I have never understood why earth's inner core is solid. Considering that the inner core is made of an iron-nickel alloy (melting point around 1350 C to 1600 C) and the temperature of the inner core is approximately 5430 C (about the temperature of the surface of the sun). Since Earth's core is nearly 3-4 times the melting point of iron-nickel alloys how can it possibly be solid? Pressure inside the Earth significantly increases the melting point of iron-nickel alloys. While the temperature at the inner core is indeed very high—higher than the melting point of iron and nickel at surface pressure—the immense pressure from the overlying layers raises the melting point of these metals. This pressure is so great that, despite the high temperature, the inner core remains solid. In contrast, the outer core, which is under less pressure, remains liquid.

The enlightening image below is of a lightning strike slowed down at 10,000 frames per second. It can be seen that the most intense flash produced from the lightening occurs in the direction from the ground up. Why does this final "ground-up" strike occur and why is it so much brighter and faster than the initial part of strike heading towards the ground? The enlightening image below is of a lightning strike slowed down at 10,000 frames per second. It can be seen that the most intense flash produced from the lightening occurs in the direction from the ground up. Why does this final "ground-up" strike occur and why is it so much brighter and faster than the initial part of strike heading towards the ground? The enlightening image below is of a lightning strike slowed down at 10,000 frames per second. It can be seen that the most intense flash produced from the lightening occurs in the direction from the ground up. Why does this final "ground-up" strike occur and why is it so much brighter and faster than the initial part of strike heading towards the ground? The final 'ground-up' strike, known as the return stroke, occurs when the negatively charged stepped leader from the cloud approaches the ground and connects with positive charges rising from objects on the surface. Once the connection is made, a powerful surge of electricity travels rapidly from the ground upward through the established channel, releasing a massive amount of energy. This upward-moving discharge is much brighter and faster than the initial leader because it involves the sudden, high-current flow that neutralizes the accumulated charges, producing the intense flash commonly associated with lightning.

But as anyone who lives in places that gets snow can tell you, the most bitter parts of winter are in January and February, not December.    Why does it get so much colder when sunlight (and the warmth it should be bringing with it) is increasing? The time lag between the winter solstice (the shortest day of the year) and the coldest days of the year is primarily due to a phenomenon called 'seasonal lag.' While the solstice marks the point at which the Northern Hemisphere receives the least amount of sunlight, the Earth and its atmosphere retain heat, and it takes time for the ground, water, and air to lose the heat they have absorbed. After the solstice, although days start getting longer and sunlight increases, the amount of heat radiated away from the Earth's surface still exceeds the amount of heat the Earth gains from the Sun. I know NASA have managed to produce soil that mimics Martian soil, however I also know how expensive it is.  My question is, what is the closest proxy to real Martian soil that I can create using readily available supplies from builders merchants, garden suppliers, chemists, and supermarkets? Martian soil, also called regolith, is different from Earth's soils because it has very low organic matter, is alkaline, and contains a lot of iron oxides (which give Mars its red color). To create a close proxy using common materials, you can mix sand (for texture), a small amount of clay (for fines), some perlite or vermiculite (for aeration and drainage), and optionally a small amount of garden lime to raise pH. To mimic the iron content, you can add red iron oxide powder (sold as ferric oxide pigment in builders’ or art suppliers).

Similarly the solstices are commonly called the "first day of summer" and "first day of winter." However in most other languages -- and even in older discussions in English -- the solstices have names like "Midsummer's Day" and "Midwinter's Day."  Is there some sound reason (e.g. typical temperatures, asymmetrical sunrise/sunset times, thermal inertia, etc.) to consider a solstice as the first day of a new season, rather than the middle? At divergent plate boundaries, tectonic plates move away from each other, typically forming mid-ocean ridges when they occur between oceanic plates (like the Mid-Atlantic Ridge). On Earth, divergent boundaries can also exist between continental plates, forming rift valleys (such as the East African Rift). However, true land-ocean divergent boundaries are not found on Earth. Divergent boundaries generally occur between plates of the same type—ocean-ocean or continent-continent. Mining the core is hardly imaginable, while the resources in Crust are more accessible available for mining: so how much is in the Crust, and at what levels of concentration? (which affects the viability of accessing it). Current scientific estimates place the total amount of uranium in Earth's crust at approximately 2.8 parts per million (ppm) by weight, equivalent to about 40 trillion kilograms (or 40 million metric tons) globally. The average concentration varies, with higher concentrations found in certain types of rocks, such as granites and sandstones. Economically viable uranium deposits—those that can be mined profitably—generally have concentrations ranging from 0.01% (100 ppm) to several percent in exceptional cases.

Instead, helium and hydrogen are rising through the atmosphere and escape into outer space.  My question is: How massive would Earth have to be so that it could keep helium in the atmosphere? 2, 5, or 10 times the actual mass? Could we, for example, compare it to Neptune or Saturn? The ability of a planet to retain helium in its atmosphere depends on its escape velocity and the temperature of the upper atmosphere. Helium, being light, escapes Earth because its thermal speed at Earth's upper atmosphere temperatures is near or above Earth's escape velocity. If Earth were about 2 times more massive (with a similar composition and temperature profile), its escape velocity would increase, but likely not enough to fully retain helium over geologic time. At 5 to 10 times Earth's mass (with corresponding increases in radius according to planetary scaling laws), the escape velocity would be high enough to significantly reduce helium loss. Gas

(Not Greenland moving to the NP) Internal processes within the Earth, such as mantle convection and mass redistribution in the mantle and core, can cause small changes in the orientation of Earth's rotation axis relative to its surface (a phenomenon called true polar wander). However, these changes are typically very gradual and limited in scale—over millions of years, the geographic North Pole can shift by several degrees, but dramatic shifts such as moving the pole to Greenland are extremely unlikely to occur due to internal activity alone. Major impacts or massive redistribution of mass (for example, by a giant asteroid) would be needed for rapid or drastic changes in the rotation axis.

We know that plates can subduct, causing one plate to be pushed into the core by another. As the plates move, the subduction continues, pushing one plate under the other.  If this process continues, logic says, the entire plate could be subducted under the other.  Has this ever happened? How would we know? Yes, entire oceanic plates can be, and have been, almost completely subducted beneath continental or other oceanic plates over geological time. Two lines of evidence support this: 1) seismic tomography (imaging Earth's interior with seismic waves) often reveals 'slabs'—cold, subducted lithosphere—sinking deep into the mantle, sometimes all the way to the core-mantle boundary; 2) studies of the rock record and plate reconstructions show that ancient plates, such as the Izanagi and Farallon plates, have largely disappeared as a result of subduction

Do we know when and where these rifting / break-up events took place? The breakup of Pangea began in the Jurassic period about 175 million years ago and occurred in stages over tens of millions of years. The first major split separated Laurasia (northern continents) from Gondwana (southern continents). The last major connections were between South America and Africa, and between Europe and North America. The South America-Africa split, forming the South Atlantic Ocean, is considered one of the final major rifting events and began around 130 million years ago and was largely complete by about 100 million years ago. In an area of frequent thunderstorms, I notice a 'crack or whoosh' sound if a strike is nearby but little or no thunder. Yet I can hear for 30 seconds or so after a distant flash when the thunder first is heard, the sound starts at a high frequency and then evolves into a deep rumble that can shake the walls. It approaches the sub-sonic. What is the relationship between the distance and the energy of the strike? Thunder sounds sharp and brief when lightning strikes nearby because the sound travels a short, direct path to your ears, allowing you to clearly hear the initial crack or bang from the rapidly expanding air. For distant lightning, the thunder travels farther and gets scattered and reflected by the atmosphere and terrain. This causes the high frequencies to arrive first and fade quickly, while the lower frequencies persist and rumble longer, sometimes approaching sub-sonic tones. The energy of the strike also affects loudness but does not significantly impact how the sound evolves with distance—the distance primarily shapes the character and duration of the thunder you hear.

The reason the Antarctic ice sheet is only a few kilometers thick, despite low but persistent precipitation rates over millions of years, is that the ice sheet is not simply accumulating indefinitely. Over time, the weight of accumulating snow compacts lower layers into ice, which then deforms and flows outward under the force of gravity. This slow movement causes the ice to spread toward the coasts, where it calves into icebergs or melts. What are these numbers? The values svp_A = 6.106, svp_B = 17.27, and svp_C = 237.3 are empirical coefficients used in the Tetens formula, which estimates the saturation vapor pressure (in hPa) of water vapor over a flat surface of liquid water as a function of temperature. The formula is: e_s(T) = svp_A * exp((svp_B * T) / (T + svp_C)), where T is the temperature in degrees Celsius. These coefficients originate from the work of O. Tetens (1930) and have been widely adopted

The great depth of the Mariana Trench compared to other trenches is primarily due to several geological mechanisms: the old, dense, and cold Pacific Plate is being subducted beneath the younger, less dense Philippine Plate. The age and density of the subducting slab allow it to sink deeper into the mantle at a steep angle. Additionally, the lack of thick sedimentary fill in the trench and the specific geometry of the plates involved allow the trench to reach greater depths than most other subduction zones.

What causes the typical red, pink or orange colors seen in sun-rises and sun-sets?  How come the sky doesn't just turn a darker blue? What causes the typical red, pink or orange colors seen in sun-rises and sun-sets?  How come the sky doesn't just turn a darker blue? What causes the typical red, pink or orange colors seen in sun-rises and sun-sets?  How come the sky doesn't just turn a darker blue? The colors seen at sunrise and sunset are different from those observed at midday because of the angle of the sun relative to the horizon. During sunrise and sunset, sunlight has to travel through a greater thickness of Earth's atmosphere. The shorter blue and violet wavelengths are scattered out of the line of sight by molecules and particles in the atmosphere, leaving the longer wavelengths—red, orange, and pink—to dominate the sky's color. That's why the sky often appears red, pink, or orange near the horizon at these times, rather than simply turning a darker blue.

If I wanted to make an air shield around the earth to contain exactly half the planet's atmosphere (by mass), how far above sea level would I have to build it?  It's slightly complicated to me because you have to take into consideration the change in pressure as well as the geometric growth of the volume of a sphere as you increase the radius.  Thank you. As altitude increases, both the pressure and density of air decrease. Atmospheric pressure at any given altitude is the weight of the air above that height per unit area. Near sea level, both the pressure and density are highest because the entire column of air is above you. To contain exactly half the atmosphere's mass below an 'air shield,' you need to find the altitude where the pressure is about half of the sea-level value (since pressure is a proxy for the weight of air above). For Earth, this altitude is approximately 5.5 kilometers (about 18,000 feet) above sea level.

Scientists who study magma, often referred to as magmatologists, investigate several specific aspects of magma. These include its chemical composition, temperature, viscosity, and the gases it contains. They also examine processes such as magma formation in the Earth's mantle or crust, its movement and storage in magma chambers, crystallization, and differentiation. Additionally, magmatologists study how magma interacts with surrounding rocks and the physical dynamics of its ascent toward the Earth's surface, which influences volcanic eruptions. Magmatology is a specialized branch of volcanology that focuses specifically on the study of magma.

When these waves encounter boundaries between different materials (such as soil and various rock types), some of the energy is reflected back to the surface. Underground sources such as springs and groundwater play a vital role in maintaining river flow during periods without rainfall. These subsurface water reserves slowly release water into streams and rivers, a process known as baseflow. Groundwater enters rivers through the riverbeds or banks, sustaining the flow when direct surface runoff from rainfall is absent. This is especially important during dry seasons or droughts, as the steady contribution from groundwater helps stabilize river levels and maintains aquatic habitats. The amount of groundwater discharge depends on local geology, rainfall history, and human extraction of groundwater.

Newton and Kepler estimated the age of the Earth by analyzing biblical chronologies, particularly genealogies found in the Book of Genesis. They used historical and religious texts to calculate the time elapsed since creation, arriving at dates such as around 4000 BCE. Their approach was common at the time, before the advent of geological and radiometric dating methods.

The percentage of SiO2 in a rock (often referred to as 'silica content') is calculated based on the total amount of SiO2 that is chemically present in the rock, regardless of the specific minerals in which it occurs. This means that SiO2 present in all minerals—whether as pure quartz or as part of silicate minerals like anorthite—is included in the total silica calculation. The standard convention in petrology is to express this value as a weight percentage (mass percent), not volume.

You are correct in your understanding. In ocean wave spectra like the Pierson-Moskowitz or JONSWAP models, S(ω) represents the energy density spectrum as a function of angular frequency ω. The units m²/(rad/s) come from the fact that S(ω)dω gives the contribution to the variance of the surface elevation (measured in meters squared) from the frequency range ω to ω + dω. Physically, integrating S(ω) over all frequencies, ∫S(ω)dω, yields the total variance (mean square surface elevation) of the sea

Given the fact that industries emit about 1.5 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide each year just in the US, why is it still such a small part of the atmosphere's composition (0.04%)? Given the fact that industries emit about 1.5 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide each year just in the US, why is it still such a small part of the atmosphere's composition (0.04%)? Although humans emit about 1.5 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide annually in the US, the total mass of Earth's atmosphere is enormous—about 5.15 quadrillion metric tons. CO2, even with large yearly emissions, still makes up a small fraction of the atmosphere's total composition (about 0.04%, or 400 parts per million). However, this seemingly small percentage is significant enough to impact the greenhouse effect and contribute to climate change, as carbon dioxide is very effective at trapping heat.

But if you scale up the Sun to it's real size compared to the Earth, you would realize that the Earth would covered in daylight ALL THE TIME. So what's going on here? If you were to scale up the Sun to its real size compared to the Earth and place it at the true relative distance, the Sun would still be much larger than the Earth, but because it is so far away, it only illuminates one hemisphere of the Earth at a time. This is why we experience day and night. The misconception comes from diagrams that show the Sun and Earth much closer in scale and distance than they actually are, sometimes making it look like the Sun would cover the entire sky. In reality, the vast distance between the Earth and Sun allows for both day and night as the Earth rotates.

Plants with a high leaf-to-trunk ratio, such as many fast-growing herbaceous plants (for example, grasses or certain leafy houseplants like the peace lily or pothos), are generally the most efficient at producing oxygen per unit of weight. This is because they have more green, photosynthetic tissue relative to non-photosynthetic structures like trunks or thick stems. In particular, aquatic plants such as duckweed (Lemna minor) and algae are among the most efficient oxygen producers relative to their biomass, since nearly all their tissue participates in photosynthesis and they have minimal support structures. My understanding of climate change is that a large increase in human-caused CO2 emissions is likely to lead to large change in the Earth's climate through a global warming effect. Scientific consensus appears to me to be that if we make similarly large reductions in CO2 emissions, we can reduce the magnitude of the consequent warming.    I also understand the climate to have many non-linear mechanisms whereby the link between emission and warming isn't at all straightforward - and so we can only talk about broad averages and so forth, rather than make specific weather predictions.    So is it always true that a reduction in emissions will - on average - cause a reduction in the global warming effect, however small? Or does the existence of tipping points or any other factor mean that only large reductions can make a difference now and any lesser reduction will be pointless?    My intuition says that smaller cuts in emissions should still be preferred to no cuts at all, as I imagine that they would reduce the peak warming and/or delay the peak for longer and/or reduce the eventual recovery time. Perhaps not by as much as we might prefer, but still better than nothing. But perhaps my intuition is off. Perhaps not by as much as we might prefer, but still better than nothing. But perhaps my intuition is off. Even small reductions in CO2 emissions lead to less greenhouse gases accumulating in the atmosphere, which slows the rate of global warming. Climate models and scientific consensus agree that incremental cuts, while individually smaller in their effect, still contribute positively to limiting the magnitude and rate of temperature increases. While tipping points and non-linear responses can make the system more unpredictable, each reduction in emissions lessens overall risk and can delay or potentially avoid the activation of certain tipping points. So, smaller cuts are indeed better than none and have a meaningful impact, even if large reductions are needed for more substantial benefits.

Geological evidence suggests that the first significant dry deserts formed during the late Paleozoic era, particularly in the Permian period (about 299–252 million years ago), when Pangaea, the supercontinent, contained vast inland areas far from the ocean, leading to arid conditions. The oldest large desert system for which there is evidence is thought to be the interior of Pangaea itself, but specific areas such as the modern Namib Desert are among the oldest existing deserts, estimated to be at least 55 million years old

What is a temperature inversion and can it trap smog/pollution?  Just as the title says, I heard about this term but am not sure how it works. What is a temperature inversion and can it trap smog/pollution?  Just as the title says, I heard about this term but am not sure how it works. A temperature inversion occurs in the atmosphere when a layer of warm air sits above a layer of cooler air near the ground. Under normal conditions, air close to the ground is warmer and rises, allowing for vertical mixing, which helps disperse pollution. During a temperature inversion, this pattern reverses and the cooler, denser air is trapped beneath the warm air, preventing the dispersion of smog and pollution. As a result, pollutants can build up near the ground, leading to poor air quality. Temperature inversions commonly occur during calm, clear nights and are often seen in valleys or basins.

Should the meteorological winter start at January 1 and end at March 31 then all seasons do exactly fit in the same year. So is there a reason why meteorologists do it this way or is it just arbitrarily chosen? Meteorological seasons are defined for ease of record-keeping and consistency in climate statistics. They align with the calendar months: winter is December–February, spring is March–May, summer is June–August, and autumn is September–November. This system makes it straightforward to compare monthly and seasonal climate data over many years. The division is not arbitrary but chosen for practicality and statistical reasons, rather than following the more variable astronomical seasonal start dates.

In the mainstream media the problem of ocean level rise is presented rather... over-simplistically - ice melts -> more water flows into the oceans -> water level rises -> continents sink.  But it is in reality a complex system, it is not like pouring water in a glass... or a lake that resides on a continental plate.  Both continental and oceanic plates float on top of the mantle as both are lighter and lower density, and both exert weight and compress the mantle.  Therefore, if water is removed from continental plates by means of melting ice, the continental plate gets lighter. And when that water migrates to the ocean, the oceanic plate gets heavier. And since for every action there is equal in force and opposite in direction reaction, it seems logical that the now heavier oceanic plate will actually push the now lighter continental plate up, to some extent compensating for or possibly even reversing the change in ocean level relative to the continental plate.  So, do the estimates account for the dynamic properties of that complex system? Glacial isostatic adjustment (GIA), also known as post-glacial rebound, is the process by which land masses that were depressed by the weight of ice sheets during the last glacial period rise after the ice has melted, while areas that had water added (such as ocean basins) may subside. Ocean level rise estimates do account for these dynamic earth processes. Scientists use complex models that include the redistribution of water mass, viscoelastic response of the mantle, and the gravitational, rotational, and deformational effects. Isn't water denser near the equator because of higher salinity? How does this impact the ocean circulation? The main factors driving thermohaline circulation in the oceans are differences in water density, which are controlled by temperature (thermo) and salinity (haline). Cold water is denser than warm water, so as water cools (especially at high latitudes, near the poles), it becomes denser and sinks, forming deep water currents. Warm, less dense water travels at the surface from the equator toward the poles. When it reaches colder regions, it cools, increases in density, and sinks, creating a global conveyor belt of ocean circulation. Near the equator, water is warmer

Atmospheric models handle species like the hydroxyl radical (OH), which have very short lifespans, by using specialized numerical techniques such as operator splitting and implicit or semi-implicit integration methods. These approaches allow the models to accurately simulate fast chemical reactions despite having time steps much larger than the microsecond-scale lifetime of OH. The numerical stability of the solution is maintained by solving the stiff equations associated with these rapid chemical processes separately from slower processes like transport. Therefore, the problem of numerical stability is not ignored; instead, it is addressed through these advanced computational methods.

The image below is included in the BBC article Amazing white rainbow snapped over Scottish moor. Apparently this phenomenon is sometimes called a "fog-bow" and is characterized by the washing out of perceived color relative to a conventional rainbow.  Is this related to the geometry (fog being far closer to the viewer) or properties of the droplets themselves? If so, which properties and why? The whiteness of a fog-bow compared to the vivid colors of a traditional rainbow is primarily due to the size of the water droplets. Fog droplets are much smaller—usually less than 0.1 mm in diameter—than raindrops. These smaller droplets cause greater diffraction and less effective color separation, which washes out the colors and produces the white or colorless appearance of a fog-bow. Therefore, it's mainly the properties of the droplets (specifically their small size) that lead to this phenomenon, rather than just the geometry.
